perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will limit the unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as the Receiving Party uses to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will attempt to limit the unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information by using what it considers a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will limit...by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care' to 'will attempt to limit...by using what it considers a reasonable degree of care' introduces ambiguity regarding the standard of care required to protect Confidential Information. The original text implies a minimum standard (reasonable degree), while the modified text makes the standard entirely subjective ('what it considers reasonable').
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modification replaces an objective 'reasonable degree of care' standard with a subjective one ('what it considers a reasonable degree of care'). This subjective standard could potentially fall below the legally required 'reasonable efforts' and thus make it harder to demonstrate that the Receiving Party took adequate steps to protect the confidential information, potentially violating CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces an objective standard of care ('reasonable degree of care') with a subjective one ('what it considers a reasonable degree of care'). The Law Explanation states that this subjective standard could fall below the legally required 'reasonable efforts' under CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By using a subjective standard, the Receiving Party may not be fulfilling their duty to maintain secrecy, potentially breaching the agreement and violating CUTSA, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will promptly return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all documents or other materials furnished by the Disclosing Party constituting Confidential Information, together with all copies thereof in the possession, custody or control of the Receiving Party, without retaining any copies. Any destruction must be confirmed by the Receiving Party to the Disclosing Party in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will return or destroy documents containing Confidential Information when feasible. Destruction may be optionally confirmed in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will promptly return' to 'will return...when feasible' creates ambiguity about the promptness and certainty of the return or destruction. Replacing 'must be confirmed' with 'may be optionally confirmed' makes the confirmation of destruction discretionary. The original obligation is clear and mandatory; the modification makes it vague and less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.04 addresses injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. The modification weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, making it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to obtain an injunction under Minnesota law. The phrase 'when feasible' undermines the mandatory nature of the return or destruction of the data, as is expected to occur according to Minnesota Law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.04 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.04 325C.03 325C.05 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.04 Version List 325C.04 ATTORNEY'S FEES. If (i) a claim of misappropriation is made in bad faith, (ii) a motion to terminate an injunction is made or resisted in bad faith, or (iii) willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party. History: 1980 c 594 s 6 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity and discretion where the original contract mandated specific actions regarding confidential information. The changed text replaces 'will promptly return' with 'will return...when feasible', and 'must be confirmed' with 'may be optionally confirmed'. This weakens the obligation and makes enforcement more difficult, potentially undermining the protections intended by trade secret laws. While the provided law snippet (Minnesota Statutes § 325C.04) focuses on attorney's fees in cases of bad faith or willful misappropriation, the perturbation explanation and law explanation suggest this change could hinder efforts to obtain injunctive relief for misappropriation under Minnesota law because the return/destruction of confidential info is no longer guaranteed.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will furnish only that portion of the Confidential Information which the Receiving Party is advised by opinion of counsel it is legally required to furnish and will exercise reasonable efforts to limit the scope and content of such disclosure and obtain confidential treatment of such Confidential Information
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will furnish the Confidential Information which legal counsel suggests is required and may try to limit the scope of such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will furnish only that portion...legally required to furnish' to 'will furnish the Confidential Information which legal counsel suggests is required' removes the objective 'legally required' standard, replacing it with the subjective 'legal counsel suggests'. Replacing 'will exercise reasonable efforts to limit the scope' with 'may try to limit the scope' makes the effort to limit the disclosure optional.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45(d)(3)(A) states that when facing a subpoena, a party must take reasonable steps to avoid disclosing protected information. Modifying the contract to state that the Receiving Party 'may try to limit the scope of such disclosure' introduces ambiguity and lessens the legal obligation to protect confidential information when facing a subpoena, contrasting with the required 'reasonable steps'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Receiving Party to furnish only the portion of Confidential Information 'legally required' and to 'exercise reasonable efforts' to limit disclosure. The changed text replaces 'legally required' with 'legal counsel suggests' and makes the effort to limit disclosure optional ('may try'). The Law Explanation references FRCP 45(d)(3)(A), stating a party must take 'reasonable steps' to protect information when facing a subpoena. By making the effort to limit disclosure optional, the changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The change introduces ambiguity and reduces the likelihood that the Receiving Party will take 'reasonable steps' to protect confidential information, creating a contradiction, as the new language does not strongly encourage reasonable steps.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will disclose Confidential Information only to its employees and to its representative whose participation is necessary or helpful to evaluate and advance to the Business Transaction and who have agreed to be bound by the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will generally disclose Confidential Information to its employees and representatives involved in evaluating the Business Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates that the receiving party can *only* disclose confidential information to employees/representatives necessary for the deal that have agreed to the terms. The changed text introduces ambiguities by removing the conditions that the employees must have agreed to the terms of the agreement and whose participation is necessary or helpful, in addition to changing 'will disclose only' to 'will generally disclose'. This creates room for broader, less controlled disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA protects trade secrets. By allowing broader disclosure to employees and representatives without requiring a specific need or agreement to confidentiality terms, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation or unauthorized use, potentially violating DUTSA's requirements for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The original text better ensures that confidential information is restricted to those who need it and who are bound by the agreement's confidentiality provisions, whereas the changed text leaves room for broad divulgement.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts DUTSA (Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act) by increasing the risk of misappropriation. The original text restricted disclosure to employees/representatives whose participation is *necessary or helpful* AND who agreed to be bound by the terms of the agreement. The changed text broadens this to *employees and representatives involved* in evaluating the Business Transaction, without the condition that they have agreed to confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines misappropriation as the disclosure or use of a trade secret 'without express or implied consent'. By removing the explicit agreement to confidentiality terms, the changed text opens the door to disclosures that are not protected by such an agreement, increasing the risk of misappropriation, thereby contradicting the need for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets under DUTSA.
